title: Neolloydia horripila
taxonomy: Neolloydia horripila is classified in the family Euphorbiaceae, which includes other species of spurges and related plants. This plant is a member of the genus Neolloydia, which is a group of cacti found in the Americas. It is further classified as a species within the subfamily Pachycereeae, which includes other species of columnar cacti. The classification of Neolloydia horripila is also recognized at the order level as Caryophyllales, a group of flowering plants that includes cacti, succulents, and other related species. At the kingdom level, it is classified as Plantae, which includes all types of plants.

intro: Neolloydia horripila, also known as the hairy-stemmed peyote or devil's head, is a species of cactus native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. It has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health issues, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, due to its antipyretic, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties. The bioactive constituents of Neolloydia horripila include alkaloids such as peyotoxine, peyotl, and lophophorine, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. In traditional herbal medicine, Neolloydia horripila is often prepared as a decoction, tincture, or infused tea, and is also used in the production of traditional Mexican medicine, such as 'peyote buttons' or 'mescal buttons'.
